q1.question: Why should I learn Hán Nôm?
q1.answer: Nearly a thousand years of Vietnamese literature, history, and poetry were recorded in Hán Nôm — from Nguyễn Du's Truyện Kiều and Nguyễn Trãi's Bình Ngô đại cáo to countless royal edicts, family genealogies, and stone inscriptions still kept in village communal houses. In the early 20th century, under French colonial rule, Quốc Ngữ (Roman script) gradually replaced Hán Nôm in schools and official documents, and the script has since fallen out of everyday use. Learning even a few dozen characters is enough to access these classical works in their original form and to feel the deeper roots of the Vietnamese language.

q2.question: What's the difference between Hán and Nôm?
q2.answer: Hán (漢) are Chinese characters borrowed as-is — Vietnamese scholars used them for centuries to write official documents, court records, and classical texts, reading them in the Sino-Vietnamese (Hán Việt) pronunciation. Nôm (喃) — meaning 'Southern script' — was invented by Vietnamese to write native words that have no Chinese equivalent. Each Nôm character typically combines a semantic component (for meaning) with a phonetic component (for sound). For example, 𢆥 (năm, 'year') combines 年 (the Hán character for 'year', read niên) for meaning with 南 (read nam) for pronunciation, since nam is close to năm in modern Vietnamese.

q3.question: I found a character I can't type. How do I look it up?
q3.answer: You have several options: draw the character with handwriting input (finger or stylus), photograph it with camera OCR — single character or full manuscript page — or look it up by radical (bộ thủ), components (ghép bộ — search by any sub-part, not just the radical), stroke count, or Unicode codepoint. Semantic search lets you describe a concept in plain Vietnamese to find related characters.

q4.question: How do I practice writing characters?
q4.answer: Build flashcard decks and study with five Quizlet-style modes: Learn (adaptive multiple-choice), Match (timed pairing), Test (mixed quiz), Recognition (self-assessed), and Writing (handwriting check). Spaced repetition (SM-2) tracks the cards you struggle with; a GitHub-style progress heatmap shows your daily activity and streak. Decks can be shared via link, and stroke-order animations teach proper writing technique.

q5.question: What classical texts are available?
q5.answer: Our classical library includes Truyện Kiều, Lục Vân Tiên, Tam Thiên Tự, Tam Tự Kinh, Luận Ngữ, Đạo Đức Kinh, and more — each with original Hán Nôm alongside modern Vietnamese translation. The reader supports horizontal and classical right-to-left vertical layouts, automatic Lục bát 6-8 column pairing for traditional Vietnamese poetry, and a rotated book-gloss mode where every character carries its Hán Việt reading. Tap any character to see meanings, pronunciations across five languages, and historical glyph variants (seal, bronze, oracle bone).

character.classA1: Sino-Vietnamese borrowing — same form, same Hán Việt reading
character.classA2: Sino-Vietnamese borrowing — same form, shifted Vietnamese reading
character.classB2: Sino-Vietnamese loan with phonetic + semantic shift
character.classC1: Phonetic loan from Hán — same form, used purely for sound
character.classC2: Phonetic loan with shifted Vietnamese reading
character.classificationLegendSource: Source: Tự Điển Chữ Nôm Dẫn Giải — Nguyễn Quốc Hùng, 2014
character.noMeanings: No Hán Nôm definition available yet
character.noReadings: No Hán Nôm pronunciation available yet
character.fanqieToneNote: Ngang + voiced initial l/m/n/ng/nh/d/v → lower register (下平)
character.fanqieSimulatedNote: Simulated based on known readings. No Guangyun data available.
character.fanqieCantoneseNote: Estimated from Cantonese reading. No Hán Việt or Guangyun data available.

stats.title: Nôm Na Việt in Numbers
stats.description: A comprehensive overview of the Hán Nôm dictionary database
stats.unicodeBrowserDesc: Explore characters by Unicode block. Click a block to browse its characters.
stats.classicalDesc: Classical Vietnamese literature in Hán Nôm script, with modern Vietnamese translations
stats.historicalScriptsDesc: Eight CJK / Hán Nôm calligraphic styles in chronological order. Type any text below — it'll render in every script. Click a card to apply that style site-wide.
stats.scriptInputPlaceholder: Type or paste Hán Nôm text…
stats.scriptDescSeal: Ancient pictographic script evolved from oracle bone writing. Standardised by Qin Shi Huang as Small Seal. Wire-thin uniform strokes, engraved on imperial seals and stone steles. Two types: Large Seal and Small Seal.
stats.scriptDescLishu: A turning point in Han-script history — simplified from Seal script. Defining feature: "silkworm-head, swallow-tail" (蠶頭燕尾) horizontal strokes with curved end-flicks. Two phases: Qin Clerical and Han Clerical.
stats.scriptDescCao: Bold, rapid brushwork. Highly abstracted strokes, sometimes a whole character in a single stroke. Difficult to read — favours art and emotion over communication.
stats.scriptDescHanh: Semi-cursive: balances Regular and Cursive — strokes flow continuously yet remain legible. Most readers of Regular script can read Running. The everyday handwriting standard.
stats.scriptDescKai: Formal script for official documents and printing — clear structure, distinct strokes. The calligraphic and publishing standard; foundation for learning the other styles.
stats.scriptDescSong: First print typeface, developed via Song-dynasty woodblock printing. Thin horizontals, thick verticals, small serifs. Ancestor of modern Mincho/Songti; standard for Hán Nôm books.
stats.scriptDescNom: Vietnamese Nôm woodblock printing tradition, used in classical literature. Songti-style adapted for Vietnamese Nôm with 6,500+ PUA codepoints for unique Nôm characters.
stats.scriptDescHei: Modern sans-serif, uniform stroke weight, descended from Bauhaus and 20th-c. East-Asian modernism. Used for headlines, signage, digital interfaces.
stats.scriptExampleSeal: Notable: 石鼓文 (Stone Drum Inscriptions), 秦始皇刻石 (Qin imperial steles)
stats.scriptExampleLishu: Notable: 曹全碑 (Cao Quan Stele), 張遷碑 (Zhang Qian Stele)
stats.scriptExampleCao: Notable: 自敘帖 (Autobiography of Huai Su), 古詩四帖
stats.scriptExampleHanh: Notable: 蘭亭集序 (Wang Xizhi, AD 353) — "the world's foremost Running script"
stats.scriptExampleKai: Notable: 顏真卿 (Yan Zhenqing), 歐陽詢 (Ouyang Xun), 柳公權 (Liu Gongquan) — "Four Tang Masters"
stats.scriptExampleSong: Notable: 宋版書 (Song-edition books) — rare antique printings
stats.scriptExampleNom: Notable: Truyện Kiều (Nguyễn Du), Lục Vân Tiên (Nguyễn Đình Chiểu)
